ppt.title: Life Processes - Session 1
ppt.presentationTitle: Understanding Life Processes: Nutrition and Respiration
ppt.presentationGoal: To introduce the concept of life processes, define nutrition types, explain autotrophic nutrition with photosynthesis, and cover basic respiration mechanisms.

licensingNotes: Use OpenRouter-generated images by default and use in-app SVG/native slide text only as a fallback.
assetSearchPlan.fallbackStrategy: Use high-contrast schematic line drawings if specific photos fail to load.

licenseChecklist: All student-facing images are royalty-free or open license.
No proprietary logos from commercial textbook publishers used without permission.
SVG diagrams created in-house for exact labeling control.
presentationWarnings: Do not introduce cellular respiration details (Krebs cycle) as this is Session 1 focusing on basic gas exchange and photosynthesis overview only.
Ensure visual clarity of stomata diagram; do not use low-resolution clip art.

learningOutcomesCovered: Distinguish between autotrophic and heterotrophic nutrition
Explain the process of photosynthesis in plants

slides.teachingBeat: Set the stage for the session, establish relevance of life processes in daily observation.
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Life Processes: Nutrition & Respiration
> Life Processes
> Session 1 of 5
- Nutrition & Respiration Basics
- What makes something alive?
- The 7 Essential Life Processes
![Life Processes: Nutrition & Respiration](http://localhost:3002/api/ppt-assets/1d969c18ad6f7da3cc7544286e185e32b0d8531c.svg)

<!-- layout: visual-first -->
<!-- purpose: title_identity -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Set the stage for the session, establish relevance of life processes in daily observation.
Purpose: Hook students with the fundamental question of life.
Explain: Life is not static; it involves continuous activities like eating, breathing, and growing. Without these, an organism dies.
Ask: 'If a rock doesn't eat or breathe, why isn't it alive? What about a plant?'
Listen for: Student mentions of growth, movement, reproduction.
Transition: Today we zoom in on two critical processes that keep us going every second.
Explain: Focus: Nutrition and Respiration
-->
slides.teacherIntent: Set the stage for the session, establish relevance of life processes in daily observation.
slides.studentTakeaway: Students understand that 'life' is defined by specific continuous biological activities.

The image should feel scientific yet accessible.

slides.teachingBeat: Activate prior knowledge and introduce the concept of metabolism.
mustTeach: Metabolism: The sum of all chemical reactions
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# What Defines 'Alive'?
> Is a car alive?
> It moves. It consumes fuel.
- But it doesn't grow or reproduce.
- Living vs Non-Living Differences
- Metabolism: The sum of all chemical reactions
![What Defines 'Alive'?](http://localhost:3002/api/ppt-assets/4f23cdd4fbb5a49b76aa296cad777cfac5fe2c90.svg)

<!-- layout: visual-first -->
<!-- purpose: lesson_hook -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Activate prior knowledge and introduce the concept of metabolism.
Purpose: Distinguish living from non-living through critical thinking.
Explain: A car 'eats' petrol and 'moves', but it cannot make its own parts, heal itself, or have offspring. Living things do all this automatically.
Ask: 'Does a fire breathe? Does a cloud grow?'
Listen for: Confusion about growth in clouds/fire vs biological growth.
Correct: Clarify that biological growth involves cell division and material synthesis (metabolism).
Board work: Write 'Metabolism = Sum of all chemical reactions' on board.
-->
slides.teacherIntent: Activate prior knowledge and introduce the concept of metabolism.
slides.studentTakeaway: Students identify that living things perform specific functions non-living things do not.
bulletPoints: But it doesn't grow or reproduce.
Metabolism: The sum of all chemical reactions
speakerNotes: Purpose: Activate prior knowledge and introduce the concept of metabolism.
Purpose: Distinguish living from non-living through critical thinking.
Explain: A car 'eats' petrol and 'moves', but it cannot make its own parts, heal itself, or have offspring. Living things do all this automatically.
Ask: 'Does a fire breathe? Does a cloud grow?'
Listen for: Confusion about growth in clouds/fire vs biological growth.
Correct: Clarify that biological growth involves cell division and material synthesis (metabolism).
Board work: Write 'Metabolism = Sum of all chemical reactions' on board.
slides.visualPlan: Comparison graphic showing a car and a plant side-by-side. The car has icons for 'Fuel', 'Movement'. The plant has icons for 'Sunlight', 'Water', 'Growth', 'Reproduction'. Use simple line art style.

assets.altText: Side-by-side comparison of a car and a plant highlighting biological vs mechanical functions.

animationHints: Animate icons appearing on the plant side only

slides.teachingBeat: Define the two fundamental modes of nutrition.
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Autotrophs vs Heterotrophs
> Self-Feeders vs Other-Feeders
> Autotrophs = Producers
- Heterotrophs = Consumers/Decomposers
- Autotrophic: Make own food (Plants, Algae)
- Heterotrophic: Depend on others (Animals, Fungi)
- Key Difference: Source of Carbon

<!-- layout: text-light -->
<!-- purpose: core_concept -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Define the two fundamental modes of nutrition.
Purpose: Establish the classification of nutrition.
Explain: Autotrophs are 'producers' because they create organic food from inorganic sources. Heterotrophs must consume this pre-made food.
Ask: 'Are mushrooms autotrophs or heterotrophs? Why?'
Listen for: Confusion about fungi; clarify that fungi lack chlorophyll and absorb nutrients (heterotrophic).
Transition: Let's look closer at how the most important group, plants, do this.
Explain: Heterotrophs = Consumers/Decomposers
-->
slides.teacherIntent: Define the two fundamental modes of nutrition.
slides.studentTakeaway: Students can classify organisms based on how they obtain food.
speakerNotes: Purpose: Define the two fundamental modes of nutrition.
Purpose: Establish the classification of nutrition.
Explain: Autotrophs are 'producers' because they create organic food from inorganic sources. Heterotrophs must consume this pre-made food.
Ask: 'Are mushrooms autotrophs or heterotrophs? Why?'
Listen for: Confusion about fungi; clarify that fungi lack chlorophyll and absorb nutrients (heterotrophic).
Transition: Let's look closer at how the most important group, plants, do this.
Explain: Heterotrophs = Consumers/Decomposers
slides.visualPlan: Clean comparison table or split card design. Left side shows a green plant with sun icon (Autotroph). Right side shows diverse animals eating (Heterotroph). Use distinct color coding: Green for Autotrophs, Orange/Red for Heterotrophs.
animationHints: Fade in left column then right column

slides.teachingBeat: Explain the inputs and outputs of photosynthesis.
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# How Plants Make Food: Photosynthesis
> Inputs -> Process -> Outputs
> Water + CO2 + Light -> Food + O2
- Raw Materials: Water + Carbon Dioxide
- Energy Source: Sunlight & Chlorophyll
- Products: Glucose (Food) + Oxygen
![How Plants Make Food: Photosynthesis](http://localhost:3002/api/ppt-assets/686cd4e2a813a38f70836ff668190a853eee5cdb.svg)

<!-- layout: concept-split -->
<!-- purpose: core_concept -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Explain the inputs and outputs of photosynthesis.
Purpose: Break down the photosynthesis equation conceptually.
Explain: Plants are like solar-powered factories. They take water from roots and CO2 from air, use sunlight captured by chlorophyll to combine them into sugar (glucose).
Ask: 'Where does the oxygen we breathe come from?'
Listen for: Student guesses.
Correct: It is a byproduct of splitting water molecules during photosynthesis. This is why plants are essential for life on Earth.
Board work: Write simplified equation: 6CO2 + 6H2O --(Sunlight/Chlorophyll)--> C6H12O6 + 6O2
-->
slides.teacherIntent: Explain the inputs and outputs of photosynthesis.
slides.studentTakeaway: Students understand that sunlight, water, and CO2 are converted to food (glucose) and oxygen.
speakerNotes: Purpose: Explain the inputs and outputs of photosynthesis.
Purpose: Break down the photosynthesis equation conceptually.
Explain: Plants are like solar-powered factories. They take water from roots and CO2 from air, use sunlight captured by chlorophyll to combine them into sugar (glucose).
Ask: 'Where does the oxygen we breathe come from?'
Listen for: Student guesses.
Correct: It is a byproduct of splitting water molecules during photosynthesis. This is why plants are essential for life on Earth.
Board work: Write simplified equation: 6CO2 + 6H2O --(Sunlight/Chlorophyll)--> C6H12O6 + 6O2
slides.visualPlan: Schematic diagram of a leaf cross-section showing arrows entering (Sunlight, CO2 from air via stomata, Water from roots) and exiting (Oxygen). Highlight chloroplasts inside cells as the site. Use bright green and yellow color palette.

assets.searchQuery: Photosynthesis process diagram leaf cross section arrows sunlight water carbon dioxide oxygen educational illustration
assets.altText: Schematic diagram of photosynthesis showing inputs and outputs in a leaf.
assets.placementHint: Centered, large enough to read labels.

animationHints: Animate arrows flowing into the leaf

slides.teachingBeat: Clarify the difference between breathing (physical) and respiration (chemical).
mustTeach: Respiration: Chemical breakdown of glucose for Energy
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Breathing vs Respiration
> Physical Act vs Cellular Process
> Glucose + Oxygen -> CO2 + Water + ENERGY
- Breathing: Physical exchange of gases
- Respiration: Chemical breakdown of glucose for Energy
- Aerobic (with O2) vs Anaerobic (without)
![Breathing vs Respiration](http://localhost:3002/api/ppt-assets/77cef63751feba800733f19b513892b3f030ef8a.svg)

<!-- layout: concept-split -->
<!-- purpose: core_concept -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Clarify the difference between breathing (physical) and respiration (chemical).
Purpose: Distinguish the physical act of breathing from cellular respiration.
Explain: Breathing is just moving air in and out. Respiration happens inside cells where glucose is burned with oxygen to release energy (ATP).
Ask: 'Why do we breathe faster after running?'
Listen for: Need more oxygen/energy.
Correct: Muscles need more ATP, so respiration rate increases, requiring more O2 intake via breathing.
Transition: Now let's check what you've learned.
-->
slides.teacherIntent: Clarify the difference between breathing (physical) and respiration (chemical).
slides.studentTakeaway: Students understand that respiration releases energy from food.
bulletPoints: Respiration: Chemical breakdown of glucose for Energy
speakerNotes: Purpose: Clarify the difference between breathing (physical) and respiration (chemical).
Purpose: Distinguish the physical act of breathing from cellular respiration.
Explain: Breathing is just moving air in and out. Respiration happens inside cells where glucose is burned with oxygen to release energy (ATP).
Ask: 'Why do we breathe faster after running?'
Listen for: Need more oxygen/energy.
Correct: Muscles need more ATP, so respiration rate increases, requiring more O2 intake via breathing.
Transition: Now let's check what you've learned.
slides.visualPlan: Split visual. Left side shows lungs expanding/contracting (Breathing). Right side shows a cell with mitochondria glowing, releasing energy sparks (Respiration). Use contrasting colors: Blue for breathing, Orange/Yellow for cellular energy.

assets.searchQuery: Human lungs diagram vs animal cell respiration mitochondria educational illustration split screen
assets.altText: Comparison of breathing (lungs) and cellular respiration (mitochondria).

animationHints: Flash mitochondria to show energy release

slides.teachingBeat: Assess immediate retention of key concepts.
mustTeach: 1. What is the main pigment for photosynthesis?
2. Name one difference between autotrophs and heterotrophs.
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Check Your Understanding
> Quick Quiz
> Think-Pair-Share Activity
- 1. Pigment?
- 2. Difference?
- 3. Oxygen purpose?
![Check Your Understanding](http://localhost:3002/api/ppt-assets/befafc86765e6fe57b2af4664a3488b04864aef9.svg)

<!-- layout: concept-split -->
<!-- purpose: quick_assessment -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Assess immediate retention of key concepts.
Purpose: Formative assessment to gauge class understanding.
Explain: Give students 60 seconds to think, then discuss with a partner for 1 minute before sharing answers.
Ask: 'Who can tell me the pigment name?'
Listen for: Chlorophyll. Ask follow-up: 'Where is it found?'
Correct: In chloroplasts of plant cells.
Board work: Write correct answers on board after discussion.
-->
slides.teacherIntent: Assess immediate retention of key concepts.

slides.teachingBeat: Consolidate learning and provide a clear takeaway.
mustTeach: Nutrition: Autotrophs make food; Heterotrophs consume it.
Photosynthesis uses sunlight to create glucose and oxygen.
Respiration releases energy from food using oxygen.
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# What We Learned Today
> Key Takeaways
> - Metabolism is key to life
- - Two types of nutrition: Auto & Hetero
- - Photosynthesis = Food + Oxygen
- - Respiration = Energy Release
![What We Learned Today](http://localhost:3002/api/ppt-assets/07b1cc01f30a09af0e0bd23f8f991fd45da82feb.svg)

<!-- layout: concept-split -->
<!-- purpose: summary -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Consolidate learning and provide a clear takeaway.
Purpose: Final wrap-up and reinforcement.
Explain: Recap the four main points. Emphasize that photosynthesis is the foundation of most food chains on Earth.
Ask: 'What would happen if all plants disappeared?'
Listen for: No oxygen, no food chain collapse.
Transition: For homework, observe a plant in your garden and note its color.
Explain: Life processes define living organisms (Metabolism).
-->
